steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The journalist claims Oscar nominations boost long-term earnings even when opening weekends are modest. The blank needs a table example showing: (1) an Oscar-nominated film with a weak opening that still earned a lot overall, compared to (2) a non-nominated film.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'the opening weekend earnings for Instructions Not Included were $7,846,426 and $904,998 for The Girl Who Played with Fire': lists two opening-weekend figures but gives no Oscar status or lifetime earnings — can't support the journalist's claim. ✗
  • Choice B — 'The Girl Who Played with Fire earned more money in its opening weekend than Amélie earned, but Amélie had higher lifetime earnings': Amélie was Oscar-nominated, opened at $136,470 (much weaker), but earned $33.2M lifetime; The Girl Who Played with Fire was not nominated, opened at $904,998 (stronger), but earned only $7.6M lifetime. This is exactly the pattern: Oscar recognition $\Rightarrow$ higher long-term earnings despite a modest opening. ✓
  • Choice C — 'both Amélie and All About My Mother were recognized by the Oscars, but Amélie had higher lifetime earnings': compares two Oscar-nominated films — no non-nominated film to illustrate the contrast. ✗
  • Choice D — 'All About My Mother was recognized by the Oscars, but Instructions Not Included, which was not recognized, had higher lifetime earnings': the non-nominated film earns more, which is the opposite of the journalist's claim. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
